metadata.summary: Agents take turns in a free-form conversation to solve problems dynamically; termination triggers on a phrase or max-turn cap; modeled on the AutoGen conversation pattern.

content.title: AutoGen-Style Conversational Multi-Agent Pattern
content.summary: Agents take sequential turns in a free-form conversation. Each agent receives a sliding window of recent conversation context and the last message, produces a response, and the conversation continues until either a termination phrase appears in the output or the max-turn cap is reached. The pattern follows the AutoGen conversation model where agents dynamically negotiate the approach rather than following a pre-defined plan.

why.p: Some tasks cannot be decomposed upfront — the solution path emerges from the dialogue itself. Dynamic tasks like debugging sessions, design reviews, or adversarial question-answering benefit from agents that respond to each other's outputs rather than executing pre-planned subtasks. The conversational pattern requires no orchestrator: agents are driven by the conversation state alone. The termination phrase and max-turn cap are the only control knobs needed to prevent infinite loops.

list.item: Dynamic tasks where the approach cannot be decomposed upfront: interactive debugging, negotiation, Socratic questioning.
Adversarial verification: one agent proposes, another critiques, the conversation converges on a validated answer.
Open-ended exploration where the conversation path itself is the output: design whiteboarding, brainstorming under constraints.
Scenarios where agents need to negotiate a shared understanding before acting — the conversation is the planning step.
Tasks with a known sequential pipeline — hierarchical or sequential execution is simpler and cheaper.
Production systems requiring auditability of every decision: conversational turn logs are harder to parse than structured assignment logs.
Tight latency budgets: free-form conversations have no natural early exit except the termination phrase; worst case = max_turns LLM calls.
Tasks where agents need a shared mutable state beyond the conversation history — the only inter-agent channel here is message content.

section.name: implementation
section.p: ConversationalAgents holds a list of agents (order determines turn order), a max_turns cap, and a termination_phrase string. The chat() method rotates through agents using modular indexing. Each turn builds a context string from the last 3 conversation entries (sliding window) to stay within context limits, then calls the current speaker. If the termination phrase appears, the conversation exits cleanly with status "completed"; otherwise it continues until max_turns.

rule.statement: Every ConversationalAgents instance MUST set both a termination_phrase and a max_turns cap. Neither alone is sufficient: the phrase may never appear (causing infinite loops without max_turns), and max_turns alone produces no semantic exit signal.
rule.rationale: Without a termination phrase, the agent has no way to signal completion — the conversation always runs to max_turns. Without max_turns, a bug or confused agent that never says the phrase causes an infinite loop.

rule.statement: The sliding context window MUST be capped (default: last 3 turns). Never pass the full conversation history to each turn prompt.
rule.rationale: Conversational patterns accumulate context linearly. Passing full history to every turn causes quadratic token growth: turn N sees N-1 prior messages. A fixed sliding window (3-5 entries) keeps per-turn cost constant.

example.title: ConversationalAgents with termination phrase and max-turn cap

antipattern.description: Echo chambers: agents trained on similar data converge on the same (possibly wrong) answer even after many turns of apparent disagreement.
antipattern.reason: Conversational diversity is only real if agents have genuinely different system prompts that frame the problem differently. Identical or near-identical system prompts produce cosmetic disagreement that resolves to the same prior. Assign clearly opposing roles (proponent vs opponent, critic vs advocate) to force genuine divergence.

antipattern.description: Passing full conversation history to every turn prompt.
antipattern.reason: Turn N sees N-1 prior messages, causing quadratic token growth. A 10-turn conversation with 200-token turns averages 1000 tokens of context per call instead of 600 with a 3-turn window — cost nearly doubles for no quality gain.

section.name: debate-variant
section.p: A common specialization of the conversational pattern is the Debate pattern: one agent argues for a position, another argues against. An independent judge agent (not exposed to either debater's system prompt) evaluates the arguments and decides. The judge uses a structured rubric rather than free-form evaluation to prevent its own prior from dominating.
section.prompt: <role>{{proponent|opponent}}</role> <topic>{{debate_topic}}</topic> <opponent_argument>{{last_message}}</opponent_argument> <instruction>Counter the opponent's argument with evidence and logic. Be specific.</instruction>

section.name: tools
section.p: Framework tools suited to the conversational pattern:

list.item: autogen (pip install pyautogen): Microsoft multi-agent conversation framework; native support for termination phrases and max_turns.
langgraph (pip install langgraph): state-machine graph for multi-agent flows; use for conversational loops with explicit state transitions.
LangGraph Cloud (SaaS): hosted, persistent multi-agent state with checkpointing — essential for long-running conversations that may be interrupted.
AutoGen Studio (OSS): visual no-code builder for AutoGen multi-agent conversation configs.
